one_line_takeaway: A provably undetectable steganographic channel for LLMs that hides an arbitrary secret payload at asymptotically optimal (linear-in-length) rate by extending CGZ watermarking with a new prefix-decodable Dynamic ECC with feedback -- powerful for provenance/metadata but, by design, not robust to text edits.
key_finding: It is possible to embed an arbitrary, key-recoverable message into LLM output with ZERO change to the output distribution (provable undetectability), and the achievable payload is asymptotically optimal: linear in response length. Empirically on GPT-2 (100 runs per length, threshold t=2), hidden bits grow linearly with response length, reaching about 17.87 bits at 100 tokens; a Llama-2-7B example encoded the 10-bit payload 'OZ' into an ordinary-looking email to a professor.
core_contribution: The paper's OWN contribution is a cryptographic steganography scheme for LLMs that hides an arbitrary secret payload (not just a 0/1 watermark bit) inside a model's response while remaining provably undetectable: without a secret key, the augmented model's output distribution is computationally indistinguishable from the original model's, even under many adaptive queries, so text quality is unchanged. It EXTENDS the prior undetectable watermarking scheme of Christ, Gunn & Zamir (CGZ, 2023) rather than reinventing it; Section 3 (the CGZ watermark, which correlates model sampling randomness with a PRF-derived key) is explicitly 'adapted in its entirety from CGZ' and is NOT this paper's contribution. The genuinely new technical tool (Section 5) is a Dynamic Error-Correcting Code with noiseless feedback: a code where the first O(k') codeword symbols decode the first k' message symbols (prefix-decodable), built over a ternary codeword alphabet {0,1,backspace} using a backspace-based correction protocol. Combined with implicit score-threshold chunking of the response, this lets the payload rate reach Theta(L) bits for a length-L response (asymptotically optimal), versus the naive Theta(L/lambda).
threat_model: Undetectability against a polynomial-time distinguisher that may adaptively query both the real model and the steganographic model with arbitrary prompts but does not hold the secret key k (Definition 2.6): the distinguishing advantage is negl(lambda). Security rests on a PRF (equivalent to one-way functions) whose inputs (unique token index i, and a high-entropy prefix r for multi-query undetectability) never repeat. The paper frames this dual-use: legitimately it extends watermark-style provenance to also encode WHO used the LLM or WHEN; as a covert channel it warns an LLM (even offline) could secretly leak session metadata (user name, time, prompt) into seemingly-clean responses. The scheme is explicitly NOT robust to edits/paraphrasing of the output (left as the main open problem), so it does not defend against an adversary who rewrites the text.
limitations: Authors state: (1) No robustness to editing/paraphrasing; a full rephrase removes the payload, and prior impossibility results (Zhang et al. 2023) apply to strong edits. This is the central open problem. (2) The empirical evaluation is explicitly a proof-of-concept only; constants were not optimized (threshold t and per-token bit usage were picked ad hoc) and the authors expect the achievable rate to improve substantially with engineering. (3) Requires the response to have Omega(lambda) empirical entropy and that entropy be roughly uniformly spread (r0-saturated); low-entropy or deterministic outputs cannot hide a payload while staying undetectable. (4) Only Algorithms 3/4 (single-query undetectability) were implemented, not the full multi-query scheme. (5) The full undetectable retriever runs in quadratic time (enumerating candidate prefixes r), though this can be reduced.

datasets_used: GPT-2 (Radford et al. 2019) with randomly chosen prompts from OpenAI's GPT-2 example-prompt webpage; 100 runs per response length (Figure 2 rate experiment)
Llama-2 7B (Touvron et al. 2023) single qualitative example encoding payload 'OZ' into an email (Figures 1 and 3); no standard benchmark dataset used
quantitative_results: Hidden payload vs response length on GPT-2 (t=2, 100 runs each): 20 tokens -> 4.19 bits; 40 -> 8.01; 60 -> 11.65; 80 -> 14.75; 100 -> 17.87 bits (approximately linear growth)

Llama-2-7B example: 2-character payload 'OZ' encoded as 10 bits (01111 11010); implementation used only the first 4 of each token's 15-bit representation when scoring, due to Llama-2's low generation entropy

Theorem 5.3 (Dynamic ECC with noiseless feedback): |Sigma|=2, |Gamma|=3, corrects any eps in (0,1/2) fraction of errors with codeword length n_k = ceil(k/(1-2eps)); linear-time encoding and correction

Theorem 6.3: for an r0-saturated response of length L, expected correctly-retrieved payload prefix is Theta(L/r0); per-code-symbol error probability bounded by 1/3, and a symbol is emitted about every b+O(1) tokens
Undetectability (Def 2.6): distinguishing advantage between real and steganographic model is negl(lambda) even under adaptive multi-query access
baselines_compared: No head-to-head empirical baseline comparison is reported; the empirical section only measures the paper's own rate

Conceptual/qualitative contrast with distortion-based message-embedding schemes (Fernandez et al. 2023; Wang et al. 2023; Yoo et al. 2023b), which the paper notes alter the response distribution and may degrade text quality, unlike this undetectable scheme
Builds directly on and generalizes CGZ (Christ, Gunn & Zamir 2023) undetectable watermarking, which it treats as the prior baseline capability (single-bit watermark) it extends to arbitrary payloads
